--

Day 4 of building a password manager with Rails 8

📦 What Is a Module, Really
Every helper I have written so far starts with module, not class, and today I finally stopped to ask why.
A module is a container for related methods and constants, but unlike a class, it cannot be instantiated. You cannot do Helper.new the way you can do User.new. Modules do not represent objects, they just group functionality together so it can be shared across the app.
User.new # ✅ works, User is a class
Helper.new # ❌ fails, Helper is a moduleThis is exactly why Rails helpers are always written as modules. A helper is not something that needs its own identity or state, it is just a bundle of methods that any view can borrow. ApplicationHelper and FlashHelper both make sense as modules because nothing about them needs to be "an instance" of anything.
⚙️ What Lives in config/initializers
The config/initializers folder holds files that run exactly once, when the app boots up. Not on every request, just once at startup, in alphabetical order.
Rails ships with a few by default, like devise.rb, and you can add your own for anything that only needs to be configured a single time, like app wide constants.
One of these files was doing something I could not parse at first glance:
ActionView::Base.field_error_proc = proc do |html_tag, _instance|
match = html_tag.to_s.match(/class\s*=\s*"([^"]*)"/)
html_tag.start_with?("<label") ? html_tag.html_safe : html_tag.to_s.gsub(match[0], "class=\"#{match[1]} input-with-errors\"").html_safe
endOnce I broke it down, it turned out to be doing one simple thing: overriding how Rails wraps invalid form fields. By default Rails wraps an errored input in a field_with_errors div, which tends to break form layouts. This code intercepts that behavior and instead just appends an input-with-errors class directly onto the existing input tag, leaving labels untouched.
The matching SCSS then does the actual visual work:
.form-control.input-with-errors {
border: 1px solid $danger;
}Together the two files turn a failed validation into a simple red border, without Rails inserting any extra wrapper divs into the markup.
🔑 Devise Path Helpers in Practice
With the user model and Devise routes in place, today was about actually wiring the navbar to react to whether someone is logged in.
devise_for :users in routes.rb quietly generates a whole set of path helpers, and today I used most of them at once:
The sign out link needed one extra detail to actually work:
<%= link_to('Sign out', destroy_user_session_path, data: { turbo_method: :delete }) %>A plain link always sends a GET request, but Devise expects a DELETE request to log someone out. turbo_method: :delete tells Turbo to intercept the click and send a DELETE request instead, which is what makes the link actually log you out rather than just navigating somewhere.
👤 Building the User Dropdown
Putting it all together, the navbar now shows two completely different states depending on user_signed_in?.
Logged in, it renders a Bootstrap dropdown with the user’s email, a link to edit the account, and the sign out link. Logged out, it just shows Sign in and Sign up links side by side.
Next up, moving past the navbar and into the actual core feature: creating and storing password entries. 🔐
